title: Where High-Energy Particles Come From and Why They Matter

lesson.learn: Cosmic radiation is a broad term for energetic particles and radiation arriving from space. The most famous part of it is cosmic rays: fast-moving protons and atomic nuclei that can come from the Sun, supernova remnants, and other extreme astrophysical environments. These particles are so energetic that they can trigger particle showers in Earth's atmosphere and even affect electronics in space. Scientists study cosmic radiation to learn about both the universe and the radiation environment around Earth.
lesson.simpler: Cosmic radiation is high-energy stuff from space. Some of it comes from the Sun, and some comes from much farther away.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Cosmic radiation is a mix of high-energy particles and radiation from space, and it reveals both astrophysical processes and space-weather risk.
lesson.deeperDive: The origin of cosmic radiation depends on the energy range. Lower-energy particles can be accelerated by the Sun and carried outward in the solar wind, while the most energetic particles usually come from violent sources such as supernova remnants, pulsars, and possibly active galactic nuclei. Because charged particles are bent by magnetic fields, tracing them back to a single source is difficult. That is why cosmic radiation is studied with a combination of detectors, spacecraft, atmospheric cascades, and background-radiation measurements. The field connects particle physics, astronomy, and space engineering in one place.
lesson.funFact: A single high-energy cosmic ray can create a whole shower of secondary particles when it hits Earth's atmosphere.
references: Reviews on Galactic cosmic rays and space radiation

people: Victor Hess - first measured cosmic rays
NASA and ESA particle physics teams
Voyager mission scientists - heliopause particle studies
High-energy astrophysicists - source and propagation research
places: Earth's atmosphere - where cosmic-ray showers form
The heliosphere - solar influence on particle flow
